message.content:
ok i have a .mcp.json, and I'm getting error:
>
> You can't sign in to this app because it doesn't comply with Google's OAuth 2.0 policy.

If you're the app developer, register the redirect URI in the Google Cloud Console.
Request details: redirect_uri=http://localhost:49848/callb

Any means to set the callback host/port?

addedBlocks:
## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dev:wrapup: Session/feature wrap-up workflow. Use when finishing work, wrapping up a session, preparing to commit, or the user says "wrap up", "wrapup", "let's ship it", or "we're done".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Fix Google OAuth redirect URI configuration

content.thinking: The user is running into an OAuth redirect URI issue with Google in Claude Code—their `.mcp.json` is configured but Google is rejecting the callback because the random port (49848) isn't registered. They're asking if there's a way to control or fix the callback host and port settings. I should look into how Claude Code handles MCP OAuth flows to give them an accurate answer about whether this is configurable.

content.text: I'll look at your `.mcp.json` and check how Claude Code handles the MCP OAuth callback port.

input.prompt:
In Claude Code (the CLI), when connecting to a remote MCP server that uses OAuth 2.0, the browser opens an authorization URL with a redirect_uri like http://localhost:49848/callback using a RANDOM ephemeral port. Google's OAuth policy rejects this because the redirect URI isn't registered in Google Cloud Console.

Question: Does Claude Code provide any way to configure/fix the OAuth callback host and port for MCP servers (so it's a stable, registerable redirect URI like http://localhost:PORT/callback)? Look for: environment variables, settings.json options, .mcp.json fields, CLI flags, or any documented mechanism. Check the actual Claude Code documentation and any built-in help (run `claude mcp --help`, `claude --help`, search docs). 

Report concretely: is there a fixed/configurable callback port? What is the exact env var or setting name if one exists? If there is none, say so clearly and note what the default behavior is. Be precise — don't speculate.

content.text:
Now I have all the information I need. Based on my research, I can provide a comprehensive answer.

## Summary

**YES, Claude Code provides a way to configure the OAuth callback host and port for MCP servers.**

### The Solution: `--callback-port` Flag

Claude Code offers the **`--callback-port`** parameter to fix the OAuth callback port, eliminating the ephemeral random port problem you described.

### Exact Usage

When adding an MCP server that requires OAuth with a pre-registered redirect URI, use:

```bash
claude mcp add --transport http --callback-port <port> <server-name> <server-url>
```

**Example for Google OAuth scenario:**
```bash
claude mcp add --transport http --callback-port 8080 my-google-server https://mcp.example.com/mcp
```

This creates a fixed callback URI of: `http://localhost:8080/callback`

### Additional OAuth Configuration Options

Claude Code also provides these related flags for complete OAuth setup:

1. **`--callback-port <port>`** - Fixed port for OAuth callback (for servers requiring pre-registered redirect URIs)
2. **`--client-id <clientId>`** - OAuth client ID for HTTP/SSE servers
3. **`--client-secret`** - Prompts for OAuth client secret (alternatively, set via `MCP_CLIENT_SECRET` environment variable)

### Configuration Methods

You can set these options in three ways:

1. **CLI flags** (e.g., `claude mcp add --callback-port 8080 --client-id xxx --client-secret ...`)
2. **Environment variable for secret**: `MCP_CLIENT_SECRET=your-secret-here`
3. **Directly in `.mcp.json`** using the `oauth` field:
   ```json
   {
     "mcpServers": {
       "my-server": {
         "type": "http",
         "url": "https://mcp.example.com/mcp",
         "oauth": {
           "callbackPort": 8080,
           "clientId": "your-client-id"
         }
       }
     }
   }
   ```

### Security Note

Client secrets are stored securely in your system keychain (macOS) or credentials file (Linux/Windows), **not** in your config files.

### Fallback Mechanism

If the browser callback still fails for any reason, Claude Code has a fallback: you can manually paste the OAuth code shown in your browser into the terminal prompt.

Sources:
- [Connect to MCP servers - Claude Code Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp-quickstart)
- [Connect Claude Code to tools via MCP - Claude Code Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp)

content.text:
Now I have all the information I need. Based on my research, I can provide a comprehensive answer.

## Summary

**YES, Claude Code provides a way to configure the OAuth callback host and port for MCP servers.**

### The Solution: `--callback-port` Flag

Claude Code offers the **`--callback-port`** parameter to fix the OAuth callback port, eliminating the ephemeral random port problem you described.

### Exact Usage

When adding an MCP server that requires OAuth with a pre-registered redirect URI, use:

```bash
claude mcp add --transport http --callback-port <port> <server-name> <server-url>
```

**Example for Google OAuth scenario:**
```bash
claude mcp add --transport http --callback-port 8080 my-google-server https://mcp.example.com/mcp
```

This creates a fixed callback URI of: `http://localhost:8080/callback`

### Additional OAuth Configuration Options

Claude Code also provides these related flags for complete OAuth setup:

1. **`--callback-port <port>`** - Fixed port for OAuth callback (for servers requiring pre-registered redirect URIs)
2. **`--client-id <clientId>`** - OAuth client ID for HTTP/SSE servers
3. **`--client-secret`** - Prompts for OAuth client secret (alternatively, set via `MCP_CLIENT_SECRET` environment variable)

### Configuration Methods

You can set these options in three ways:

1. **CLI flags** (e.g., `claude mcp add --callback-port 8080 --client-id xxx --client-secret ...`)
2. **Environment variable for secret**: `MCP_CLIENT_SECRET=your-secret-here`
3. **Directly in `.mcp.json`** using the `oauth` field:
   ```json
   {
     "mcpServers": {
       "my-server": {
         "type": "http",
         "url": "https://mcp.example.com/mcp",
         "oauth": {
           "callbackPort": 8080,
           "clientId": "your-client-id"
         }
       }
     }
   }
   ```

### Security Note

Client secrets are stored securely in your system keychain (macOS) or credentials file (Linux/Windows), **not** in your config files.

### Fallback Mechanism

If the browser callback still fails for any reason, Claude Code has a fallback: you can manually paste the OAuth code shown in your browser into the terminal prompt.

Sources:
- [Connect to MCP servers - Claude Code Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp-quickstart)
- [Connect Claude Code to tools via MCP - Claude Code Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp)

content.thinking:
The real problem is that your `.mcp.json` specifies a `redirectUri`, but Claude Code is ignoring it and using a random ephemeral port instead—which means either the field name is wrong or it's not being read at all. The agent indicates the correct config should use `oauth.callbackPort` as a number along with the `--callback-port` flag, not `redirectUri`.

There's also a critical path mismatch: Claude Code hardcodes the callback path as `/callback`, not `/oauth/callback`, so you need to register `http://localhost:<port>/callback` in Google Cloud Console, not your current path. The host must be `localhost` (not `127.0.0.1`) since Google requires an exact match.

content.text:
Found it. Your config uses a field name Claude Code doesn't recognize, which is why it's falling back to a random ephemeral port.

## The problem

Your `.mcp.json` has:
```json
"redirectUri": "http://127.0.0.1:6274/oauth/callback"
```

But Claude Code doesn't read a `redirectUri` field — it only takes a **port** via `oauth.callbackPort` (or the `--callback-port` flag). Since it doesn't find a recognized port setting, it picks a random one (`49848`), which Google rejects.

Also note: Claude Code controls **only the port** — the host is always `localhost` and the path is always `/callback`. You cannot set `/oauth/callback`. The redirect URI will always be exactly:

```
http://localhost:<port>/callback
```

## The fix

**1. Change your `.mcp.json`** to use `callbackPort` instead of `redirectUri`:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "chat": {
      "type": "http",
      "url": "https://chatmcp.googleapis.com/mcp/v1",
      "oauth": {
        "clientId": "REDACTED.apps.googleusercontent.com",
        "callbackPort": 6274
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**2. Register the matching redirect URI in Google Cloud Console** (APIs & Services → Credentials → your OAuth client → Authorized redirect URIs):

```
http://localhost:6274/callback
```

It must match exactly — `localhost` (not `127.0.0.1`), port `6274`, path `/callback`.

## Two things to flag

- **Your client secret is in plaintext** in `.mcp.json` (`GOCSPX-…`). Claude Code stores secrets in the system keychain instead — set it via `MCP_CLIENT_SECRET` env var or the `--client-secret` prompt, and remove it from the file. And since you just pasted a real client secret here, you should **rotate it** in the Cloud Console.
- If you'd rather configure via CLI than hand-edit JSON:
  ```bash
  claude mcp add --transport http --callback-port 6274 \
    --client-id REDACTED.apps.googleusercontent.com \
    --client-secret \
    chat https://chatmcp.googleapis.com/mcp/v1
  ```

Want me to apply the `.mcp.json` edit (switching to `callbackPort` and dropping the secret)?
